title: Witscript 3: A Hybrid AI System for Improvising Jokes in a Conversation
research_questions: Can a hybrid neural-symbolic system improvise a variety of contextually integrated jokes in conversation using multiple joke production mechanisms?

Does Witscript 3 produce higher-quality conversational jokes than a GPT-3 baseline that is simply prompted to be funny and than human Topical-Chat responses not produced with a special attempt to be funny?

methods_proposed.name: Witscript 3
methods_proposed.summary: A neural-symbolic hybrid AI system that uses GPT-3 text-davinci-002 without fine-tuning to execute symbolic humor algorithms through prompt chaining. It generates three joke candidates using wordplay, common-sense knowledge, and a proprietary third mechanism, then uses GPT-3 to select the candidate that seems funniest.

methods_proposed.name: GPT-LOL
methods_proposed.summary: A simple baseline joke generator consisting of GPT-3 text-davinci-002 prompted with “You want to be funny. Respond to this: [The sentence].” The paper uses temperature 0.7 and Top P 1.0.

humor_theories.detail: The paper says one humor algorithm is derived from the Surprise Theory of Laughter and specifies that a monologue-type joke has a topic, an angle, and a punch line.

The Basic Joke-Writing Algorithm is operationalized as five steps: select a topic, select two topic handles, generate associations, create a punch line, and generate an angle between the topic and punch line.

evaluation_methods.detail: Fifteen Amazon Mechanical Turk workers rated each of 39 input-response pairs, in random order, on a 1–4 scale: 1 = not a joke, 2 = almost a joke, 3 = a joke, 4 = a very good joke. Workers had to be located in the United States and have a HIT Approval Rate greater than or equal to 95%; the evaluation yielded 585 ratings.

headline_results.finding: Witscript 3 achieved the highest overall mean rating among the three evaluated sources.
GPT-LOL achieved a lower overall mean rating than Witscript 3.
The Human responses had the lowest overall mean rating.
Witscript 3 responses were rated as “a joke” or “a very good joke” most often.
GPT-LOL responses were rated as “a joke” or “a very good joke” less often than Witscript 3 responses.
Human responses were rated as “a joke” or “a very good joke” least often.

key_findings: Witscript 3 is a hybrid of symbolic humor algorithms and GPT-3 text-davinci-002, using separate LLM calls for successive joke-writing steps.
The system generates three joke candidates using different mechanisms—wordplay, common-sense knowledge, and a proprietary third mechanism—then asks GPT-3 to choose the funniest candidate.
In the AMT evaluation, Witscript 3 had a mean rating of 2.36, compared with 1.96 for GPT-LOL and 1.84 for Human responses.
Witscript 3 responses were judged to be jokes 44.1% of the time, compared with 33.8% for GPT-LOL and 23.6% for Human responses.
The author argues that these results indicate a hybrid LLM-plus-symbolic-humor-algorithm approach can yield a higher-quality joke generator than an LLM merely prompted to be funny.
limitations: The evaluation used only 13 input sentences selected from Topical-Chat.
The human comparison responses came from Topical-Chat workers who were not making any special attempt to be funny.
The third joke production mechanism is described only as proprietary, so its internal method is not specified in the paper.
Witscript 3 was still judged to be a joke or very good joke only 44.1% of the time, and the paper says future work will explore ways to generate jokes more consistently.

artifacts.other: The paper states that the proprietary Witscript 3 software is available for license.

human_samples.recruitment: Hired via AMT; eligibility requirements were location in the United States and HIT Approval Rate >= 95%.
human_samples.observations: Each of 39 input-response pairs was evaluated by 15 AMT workers, yielding 585 ratings. Ratings used a 1–4 scale: 1 = not a joke, 2 = almost a joke, 3 = a joke, 4 = a very good joke.
human_samples.detail: The 39 pairs consisted of 13 responses from each source: Human, GPT-LOL, and Witscript 3. Pairs were put in a random order and presented in that same order to all evaluators, who were not told how the pairs had been obtained.

human_samples.population: AMT workers whose conversations are included in Amazon's Topical-Chat dataset
human_samples.recruitment: The paper describes Topical-Chat as comments exchanged by pairs of AMT workers asked to have coherent and engaging conversations based on topical reading material; it does not report the original dataset contributor count.
human_samples.observations: The study selected 13 input sentences from Topical-Chat and used the recorded response from the AMT worker as the Human response for each selected sentence.
human_samples.detail: For the Human baseline, the author took the entire response up until the AMT worker changed the subject, then corrected capitalization, spelling, and punctuation. The number of unique Topical-Chat workers represented in the 13 Human responses is not reported.

judges.name: AMT evaluators
judges.detail: Primary evaluators of response humorousness/joke status. They rated all 39 input-response pairs on the 1–4 joke-quality scale and were not told how the pairs had been obtained.

judges.name: GPT-3 text-davinci-002
judges.detail: Within Witscript 3, the GPT-3 API was called with a prompt to determine which of three joke candidates seemed funniest; that candidate was output as Witscript 3's response. This was generation selection, not the final human evaluation.

judges.name: Joe Toplyn
judges.detail: The author selected and standardized the 13 Topical-Chat sentences using stated criteria, including the author's judgment about whether a sentence could be responded to with a joke relatively easily. The author also standardized Human responses by correcting capitalization, spelling, and punctuation.

generation_selection.scope: GPT-LOL responses for the 13 selected Topical-Chat input sentences
generation_selection.n: 1 response used: the first response output by the system for each input
generation_selection.detail: GPT-LOL is text-davinci-002 prompted with: “You want to be funny. Respond to this: [The sentence].” Temperature was 0.7 and Top P was 1.0. No best-of-N selection is reported.

generation_selection.scope: Witscript 3 responses for the 13 selected Topical-Chat input sentences
generation_selection.detail: For each input, Witscript 3 created three punch-line/joke candidates via a wordplay mechanism, a common-sense-knowledge mechanism, and a third proprietary mechanism. It then called the GPT-3 API with a prompt to determine which of the three joke candidates seemed funniest and output that candidate.
These were pre-existing human conversational responses from the Topical-Chat dataset, not generated in the experiment. The author selected the response span up until the worker changed the subject.

model_provenance.name: OpenAI GPT-3
model_provenance.detail: Used without fine-tuning by Witscript 3 and by the GPT-LOL baseline. The paper reports the model identifier text-davinci-002 and states it has 175B parameters, but does not report an API inference date or other snapshot metadata.

model_provenance.name: Witscript 3
model_provenance.detail: Hybrid neural-symbolic system using symbolic humor algorithms, GPT-3 API prompt chaining, non-GPT NLP tools for the wordplay candidate, and a third proprietary GPT-3-powered joke production mechanism. No software version/build is reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-LOL
model_provenance.detail: Baseline joke generator created by the author using GPT-3 text-davinci-002 with the reported prompt and sampling settings. No separate system version or inference date is reported.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 1 response used per input: the first response output by the system
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Temperature = 0.7; Top P = 1.0; prompt: “You want to be funny. Respond to this: [The sentence].”
inference_budgets.detail: The paper does not report max tokens, stop sequences, number of completions requested, random seed, latency, or cost.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: A separate GPT-3 API call was made for each step, but the exact total number of calls per input is not fully reported.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 3 final joke candidates before selection; 1 selected output per input
inference_budgets.detail: Reported GPT-3 uses include selecting topic handles, generating associations, creating the common-sense-knowledge candidate, powering a third proprietary mechanism, generating an angle/joke candidate for each punch line, and selecting the funniest of the three candidates. The wordplay candidate is created without GPT-3 using unspecified NLP tools.

human_baselines.scope: Response humorousness comparison against Human Topical-Chat responses
human_baselines.description: The Human baseline consisted of the AMT worker response recorded in Topical-Chat for each selected input sentence, edited only for capitalization, spelling, and punctuation and truncated when the worker changed the subject.
human_baselines.detail: Human and AI responses were evaluated together by the same AMT evaluators under source-blind conditions, but the production conditions were not materially matched: the Topical-Chat workers were asked to have coherent and engaging conversations and, as the paper notes, were not making any special attempt to be funny.

contamination_risk.basis: The evaluation prompts and Human baseline responses came from the public Topical-Chat dataset, cited as a 2019 dataset and available from Kaggle. The evaluated LLM was GPT-3 text-davinci-002, and the paper provides no decontamination test, training-overlap check, or held-out/private item creation. The task is open-ended joke generation rather than reproducing benchmark labels, which limits but does not remove risk because the selected public sentences or conversations could have appeared in model training data.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Witscript 3's actual emitted responses, and therefore their subsequent human ratings, depend on GPT-3 selecting the funniest among three internally generated candidates. The headline comparison of Human, GPT-LOL, and Witscript 3 is nevertheless determined by AMT ratings, not by LLM evaluation.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: 15 AMT evaluators rated all 39 input-response pairs, yielding 585 human ratings; evaluators were not told how the pairs had been obtained.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: GPT-3 functions as an internal candidate selector for Witscript 3. It is not used to score the final outputs in Table 1 or Table 2.

reporting_gaps: No OpenAI API inference date, deployment snapshot beyond the text-davinci-002 model identifier, or random seed is reported.
Witscript 3's prompt templates, token limits, max_tokens, stop sequences, and sampling parameters are not reported.
The exact number of GPT-3 API calls per Witscript 3 input is not fully specified, especially for association generation, angle generation, and the proprietary third mechanism.
The third Witscript 3 joke production mechanism is described only as proprietary, and the non-GPT NLP tools used for the wordplay candidate are not named or versioned.
No compute cost, API cost, runtime, or latency is reported for GPT-LOL or Witscript 3.
Evaluator demographics beyond AMT platform, U.S. location, and HIT Approval Rate >= 95% are not reported; language proficiency is not separately reported.
The number of unique Topical-Chat workers represented in the 13 Human baseline responses is not reported.
No inter-rater reliability, confidence intervals, statistical significance tests, or effect-size analyses are reported for the human ratings.
The paper does not discuss possible training-data contamination or overlap between Topical-Chat and GPT-3/text-davinci-002 training data.
No evaluator training, attention checks, or quality-control exclusions are reported beyond the AMT eligibility filters.
